title: Depth-First Search
code: static void Dfs(int u, Dictionary<int, List<int>> adj, bool[] seen, List<int> order)
Dfs(v, adj, seen, order);  // recurse deeper

frames.caption: DFS from A. Unlike BFS (a FIFO queue, level by level), DFS dives as deep as possible down one branch, then backtracks — using a stack, here the call stack itself.
frames.note: DFS goes as far down one path as it can before backing up. It is naturally recursive: each call frame on the program stack IS the DFS stack. It does NOT find shortest paths, but it is the right tool for connectivity, cycle detection, and topological ordering. We will watch the stack grow as we descend and shrink as we backtrack.

frames.caption: Enter A: mark visited, add to order. It is the start vertex. Now scan its neighbors: [B, C].
frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. DFS commits to this branch: before looking at any sibling, it recurses into the first unvisited neighbor, plunging as deep as the graph allows. That dive-first discipline is the whole difference from BFS.

frames.caption: A's neighbor B is unvisited → recurse into B, going one level deeper.
frames.note: This neighbor is unvisited, so DFS recurses into it RIGHT NOW — pushing a new frame on the call stack and going one level deeper before its siblings get a turn. The edge we follow is a tree edge (the backbone DFS carves out). This is why DFS reaches D before it ever looks at C: depth beats breadth.

frames.caption: Enter B: mark visited, add to order. We reached it from A. Now scan its neighbors: [D, E].
frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. DFS commits to this branch: before looking at any sibling, it recurses into the first unvisited neighbor, plunging as deep as the graph allows. That dive-first discipline is the whole difference from BFS.

frames.caption: B's neighbor D is unvisited → recurse into D, going one level deeper.
frames.note: This neighbor is unvisited, so DFS recurses into it RIGHT NOW — pushing a new frame on the call stack and going one level deeper before its siblings get a turn. The edge we follow is a tree edge (the backbone DFS carves out). This is why DFS reaches D before it ever looks at C: depth beats breadth.

frames.caption: Enter D: mark visited, add to order. We reached it from B. Now scan its neighbors: [(none)].
frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. DFS commits to this branch: before looking at any sibling, it recurses into the first unvisited neighbor, plunging as deep as the graph allows. That dive-first discipline is the whole difference from BFS.

frames.caption: All of D's neighbors are done — D's call returns (backtrack), back up to B.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex is handled, so its recursive call RETURNS — we pop its frame and back up to the parent that called it ("black": fully finished). Backtracking is what lets DFS try the next branch. Across the run each vertex is entered once and each edge inspected once: O(V + E) time, O(V) stack depth.

frames.caption: B's neighbor E is unvisited → recurse into E, going one level deeper.
frames.note: This neighbor is unvisited, so DFS recurses into it RIGHT NOW — pushing a new frame on the call stack and going one level deeper before its siblings get a turn. The edge we follow is a tree edge (the backbone DFS carves out). This is why DFS reaches D before it ever looks at C: depth beats breadth.

frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. DFS commits to this branch: before looking at any sibling, it recurses into the first unvisited neighbor, plunging as deep as the graph allows. That dive-first discipline is the whole difference from BFS.

frames.caption: All of E's neighbors are done — E's call returns (backtrack), back up to B.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex is handled, so its recursive call RETURNS — we pop its frame and back up to the parent that called it ("black": fully finished). frames.caption: All of B's neighbors are done — B's call returns (backtrack), back up to A.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex is handled, so its recursive call RETURNS — we pop its frame and back up to the parent that called it ("black": fully finished). Backtracking is what lets DFS try the next branch. Across the run each vertex is entered once and each edge inspected once: O(V + E) time, O(V) stack depth.

The edge we follow is a tree edge (the backbone DFS carves out). This is why DFS reaches D before it ever looks at C: depth beats breadth.

frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. frames.note: On ENTERING a vertex we mark it visited immediately (this is "gray" — on the recursion stack) so we never expand it twice. frames.caption: All of C's neighbors are done — C's call returns (backtrack), back up to A.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex is handled, so its recursive call RETURNS — we pop its frame and back up to the parent that called it ("black": fully finished). Backtracking is what lets DFS try the next branch. Across the run each vertex is entered once and each edge inspected once: O(V + E) time, O(V) stack depth.

frames.caption: All of A's neighbors are done — A's call returns (backtrack), the start frame.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex is handled, so its recursive call RETURNS — we pop its frame and back up to the parent that called it ("black": fully finished). Backtracking is what lets DFS try the next branch. Across the run each vertex is entered once and each edge inspected once: O(V + E) time, O(V) stack depth.

frames.caption: Stack is empty — DFS is complete. Visit order: A, B, D, E, C, F (B's whole branch before C, exactly as DFS descends).
frames.note: Every reachable vertex was entered once and every edge inspected once: O(V + E) time, O(V) stack space. DFS gave A, B, D, E, C, F — depth-first, not level-by-level. This branch-first order is what makes DFS the natural fit for connectivity, cycle detection (back edge into a gray node), and topological sort (reverse finish order) — places where BFS has nothing to offer.
